title: Cat Circular Hair Loss with Red Ring: Causes and Veterinary Triage
meta_desc: Learn about circular hair loss and red rings in cats. Discover why this condition is low urgency and how a photo assists in veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Circular hair loss with a red ring is typically ringworm, a contagious fungal infection that requires isolation and a routine veterinary appointment. Taking a triage photo helps your veterinarian confirm the diagnosis and provide immediate environmental safety protocols.

What causes circular hair loss with a red ring on my cat?

When a cat develops a circular patch of hair loss accompanied by a red, scaly, or crusty outer ring, it is most frequently associated with Dermatophytosis, more commonly known as ringworm. Despite the name, ringworm is a fungal infection, not a parasite. The fungus feeds on the keratin in the hair and skin, leading to the characteristic brittle fur and circular lesions. Other possibilities include localized allergic reactions or feline acne, but the specific 'ring' pattern is a classic indicator of fungal growth.

Is circular hair loss with a red ring a veterinary emergency for my cat?

  • The urgency level for this condition is categorized as Low; while not life-threatening, it is highly contagious to humans and other pets.
  • Isolate your cat from other household members immediately to prevent the spread of this zoonotic disease.
  • Schedule a standard veterinary appointment for a definitive diagnosis and treatment plan.
  • Seek immediate emergency care only if the skin appears severely infected with pus, or if the cat shows systemic signs like extreme lethargy or loss of appetite.

Why should I take a triage photo of my cat’s skin lesion?

  • A clear, well-lit photo allows a veterinary professional to visually inspect the lesion's margins and the condition of the surrounding skin.
  • Photos help distinguish between a fungal infection and other skin issues like "hot spots" or flea-bite dermatitis.
  • Sharing a photo enables your vet to provide specific instructions on environmental cleaning and isolation protocols even before you step into the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, dermatophytosis, most commonly caused by Microsporum canis, can present with focal alopecia, scaling, and crusting, typically around the ears, face, or extremities. While a Wood's lamp can be used for a tentative diagnosis, a definitive diagnosis requires a dermatophyte test medium (DTM) culture. Clinical signs of acquired hair loss are varied and may be focal, multifocal, symmetric, or generalized, with inflammatory changes such as hyperpigmentation, erythema, scaling, and pruritus. Furthermore, demodicosis can cause focal alopecia on the head and neck, and in generalized cases, alopecia, crusting, and potential secondary pyoderma may be present. Medical evaluation and dermatophyte cultures are essential to rule out concomitant conditions and underlying immunosuppressive or metabolic diseases.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 874)

faqs.question: Can humans catch ringworm from cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, ringworm is zoonotic, meaning it can be transmitted from animals to people. It is important to wash your hands thoroughly after touching your cat and to clean bedding frequently.

faqs.question: How do veterinarians treat circular hair loss?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves a combination of topical antifungal ointments, specialized medicated baths, and sometimes oral antifungal medications for more widespread cases.

faqs.question: Should I use over-the-counter human creams on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid applying any ointments until a vet has examined the area. Some human medications contain ingredients that are toxic to cats if they lick the area.
